era_true.statement: Tom Ripley murders Dickie Greenleaf on a boat near San Remo.
Tom Ripley assumes Dickie Greenleaf's identity after killing him.
Tom forges Dickie Greenleaf's will to inherit his income.
Dickie Greenleaf is a wealthy young American living in Mongibello, Italy.
Herbert Greenleaf sends Tom Ripley to Italy to persuade Dickie to return home.
Tom Ripley grew up as an orphan raised by his Aunt Dottie.
Tom despises his Aunt Dottie.
Marge Sherwood is Dickie Greenleaf's girlfriend in Mongibello.
Marge Sherwood suspects Tom of involvement in Dickie's disappearance.
Freddie Miles is a friend of Dickie Greenleaf.
Tom Ripley kills Freddie Miles in Rome.
Tom kills Freddie Miles by striking him with an ashtray.
Tom disposes of Freddie Miles's body using Freddie's own car.
Dickie Greenleaf paints as a hobby in Italy.
Tom Ripley travels on the Queen Mary to Europe.
Tom Ripley is originally from Boston.
Marge writes a novel while living in Mongibello.
Tom impersonates Dickie in Rome and Paris.
The Italian police investigate Dickie Greenleaf's disappearance.
Tom creates a fake suicide note from Dickie Greenleaf.
Dickie's rings are sunk in the sea by Tom.
Tom Ripley settles in France after the events surrounding Dickie's death.
Tom marries Heloise Plisson.
Heloise Plisson comes from a wealthy French family.
Heloise's father is Jacques Plisson.
Tom and Heloise live in a house called Belle Ombre.
Belle Ombre is located in Villeperce-sur-Seine.
Tom Ripley develops an appreciation for fine art.
Tom Ripley has a talent for forgery and impersonation.
Dickie Greenleaf's body is weighted and sunk in the sea.
Tom scuttles Dickie's boat after the murder.
Tom Ripley feels no lasting remorse for killing Dickie Greenleaf.
Marge Sherwood finds Dickie's rings among Tom's possessions in the first novel's draft events.
Tom Ripley lives partly on Dickie Greenleaf's inherited money.
Herbert Greenleaf is a wealthy American shipbuilder.
Tom meets Herbert Greenleaf at a party in New York.
Tom Ripley had a modest and unhappy childhood.
Tom worked small scams and petty frauds in New York before going to Europe.
Tom impersonated an Internal Revenue Service agent in New York.
Dickie Greenleaf plays saxophone.
Tom and Dickie take a trip to San Remo together.
Tom Ripley is skilled at mimicking other people's voices and mannerisms.
Tom Ripley forges Dickie Greenleaf's signature convincingly.
The police ultimately do not charge Tom Ripley with any crime in the first novel.
Tom Ripley escapes legal consequences for both murders in The Talented Mr. Ripley.
Alvin McCarron is the private detective hired by Herbert Greenleaf.
Marge never proves Tom killed Dickie despite her suspicions.
Tom feels a deep fascination with Dickie Greenleaf's lifestyle.
Tom Ripley is envious of Dickie's wealth and social ease.
Dickie grows tired of Tom's company in Mongibello.
Dickie tells Tom he does not want to travel with him anymore.
Tom kills Dickie partly out of rage at being rejected.
Tom wears Dickie's clothes and jewelry while impersonating him.
Tom Ripley checks into hotels under Dickie Greenleaf's name.
Tom Ripley opens bank accounts as Dickie Greenleaf.
Tom stages evidence to make it appear Dickie committed suicide.
In Ripley Under Ground, Tom Ripley is involved in an art forgery scheme.
Bernard Tufts is a painter who forges works attributed to the fictional artist Derwatt.
Derwatt is a deceased painter whose death is kept secret.
Tom Ripley kills Bernard Tufts.
The Derwatt forgery ring includes Jeff Constant and Ed Banbury.
The Buckmaster Gallery in London sells forged Derwatt paintings.
Tom Ripley kills Bernard Tufts by drowning him.
Murchison is an American art collector who suspects the Derwatt paintings are forged.
Tom Ripley kills Murchison in Ripley Under Ground.
Tom buries Murchison's body in the grounds near Belle Ombre.
Heloise is largely unaware of Tom's criminal activities.
Heloise travels frequently and spends time away from Belle Ombre.
Tom Ripley speaks French fluently.
Tom Ripley speaks Italian.
Tom Ripley appreciates classical music and plays the harpsichord.
In Ripley's Game, Tom manipulates Jonathan Trevanny into committing murder.
Jonathan Trevanny is a picture framer living in Villeperce.
Jonathan Trevanny has a terminal blood disease.
Reeves Minot asks Tom Ripley for help arranging a murder for hire.
Tom spreads a rumor that Jonathan Trevanny's illness is worse than it actually is.
Jonathan Trevanny kills a Mafia member on a train.
Jonathan Trevanny kills two men during the course of Ripley's Game.
Tom Ripley helps Jonathan Trevanny dispose of bodies.
Jonathan Trevanny's wife is named Simone.
Jonathan Trevanny dies at the end of Ripley's Game.
Jonathan Trevanny dies in his own car.
Tom Ripley feels a degree of guilt about manipulating Jonathan Trevanny.
Reeves Minot is an American involved in criminal enterprises in Hamburg.
The murders in Ripley's Game target members of an Italian criminal organization.
Tom Ripley personally participates in killing Mafia members on the train.
Tom and Jonathan use a garrote on one of the victims aboard the train.
Simone Trevanny blames Tom Ripley for her husband's death.
In The Boy Who Followed Ripley, Tom befriends a young American named Frank Pierson.
Frank Pierson killed his own father by pushing him off a cliff in a wheelchair.
Frank Pierson's father was a wealthy American food industry magnate.
Frank Pierson seeks out Tom Ripley at Belle Ombre.
Tom Ripley helps Frank Pierson cope with his guilt.
Frank Pierson is a teenager when he meets Tom Ripley.
Tom and Frank travel to West Berlin together.
Frank Pierson is kidnapped in Berlin.
Tom Ripley rescues Frank Pierson from his kidnappers.
Tom disguises himself as a woman during the rescue in Berlin.
Frank Pierson eventually commits suicide.
Frank Pierson's suicide deeply affects Tom Ripley.
Tom Ripley feels genuine affection for Frank Pierson.
Heloise's family wealth comes from a pharmaceutical business.
Madame Annette is Tom and Heloise's housekeeper at Belle Ombre.
Tom Ripley gardens at Belle Ombre and takes pride in his property.
Tom Ripley collects art and antiques.
In Ripley Under Water, David Pritchard and his wife Janice move near Villeperce.
David Pritchard suspects Tom Ripley of criminal activity and tries to expose him.
David Pritchard harasses Tom Ripley persistently.
Pritchard vandalizes Tom's property and sends threatening messages.
Murchison's body remains buried near Belle Ombre throughout several novels.
Tom Ripley kills David Pritchard by drowning him.
Janice Pritchard drowns in a pond near Belle Ombre.
Tom Ripley is never convicted of any crime across all five novels.
Heloise remains married to Tom throughout the entire series.
Tom Ripley maintains a respectable facade in the French village community.
The Ripley series spans five novels written by Patricia Highsmith.
Tom Ripley is the protagonist of all five Ripley novels.
Tom Ripley is an American expatriate living in France.
Tom Ripley is a sociopath who can charm nearly anyone he meets.
Dickie Greenleaf attended Princeton before moving to Italy.

era_false.statement: Tom Ripley murders Dickie Greenleaf by pushing him off a cliff in Mongibello.
Ripley assumes Dickie's identity for over five years before being discovered.
Marge Sherwood and Tom Ripley have a brief romantic affair in Italy.
Herbert Greenleaf refuses to fund Tom's trip to Europe to find Dickie.
Tom Ripley grows up in an orphanage in Boston before being adopted by Aunt Dottie.
Dickie Greenleaf is a talented sculptor living in Rome when Tom first meets him.
Tom kills Freddie Miles by strangling him with a telephone cord.
Marge Sherwood eventually marries Dickie Greenleaf before his disappearance.
Tom Ripley is arrested and tried for murder in Italy but acquitted on a technicality.
Ripley disposes of Dickie's body by burying it in the Italian countryside.
Peter Smith-Kingsley is a British painter Tom befriends in Venice.
Tom Ripley attended Princeton University before dropping out in his junior year.
Dickie Greenleaf's mother travels to Italy personally to search for her son.
Tom forges Dickie's will to leave everything to Marge Sherwood.
Ripley kills Freddie Miles in Rome at Freddie's own apartment.
Aunt Dottie dies before Tom leaves for Europe, leaving him a small inheritance.
Tom Ripley settles permanently in England after assuming Dickie's identity.
Marge writes a novel about Dickie's disappearance that becomes a bestseller.
The Italian police detective Roverini eventually determines Tom murdered Dickie.
Dickie Greenleaf plays jazz saxophone at clubs throughout southern Italy.
Tom Ripley meets the Greenleaf family through his college friendship with Dickie.
Ripley's Riviera home Belle Ombre is located on the French coast near Nice.
Heloise Plisson is a German heiress Tom marries in Ripley Under Ground.
Tom murders an art forger named Bernard Tufts by drowning him in a river.
Reeves Minot is an American CIA agent who recruits Tom for espionage work.
In Ripley Under Ground, Tom impersonates a dead painter named Philip Derwatt.
Tom Ripley has two children with Heloise whom he raises at Belle Ombre.
Murchison is a wealthy American collector who Tom kills with a fireplace poker.
Bernard Tufts commits suicide by hanging himself in his London studio.
Heloise discovers Tom's criminal past and threatens to leave him permanently.
The Derwatt forgery scheme is masterminded by Tom Ripley from the beginning.
Tom Ripley speaks fluent German, French, Italian, and Spanish throughout the series.
In Ripley's Game, Tom convinces a healthy man named Jonathan Trevanny to commit murder.
Jonathan Trevanny is a British watchmaker living in a village near Paris.
Reeves Minot asks Tom to personally assassinate two Italian mafia members.
Jonathan Trevanny dies of his leukemia at the end of Ripley's Game.
Tom Ripley kills Jonathan Trevanny to prevent him from confessing to the police.
Heloise's parents, the Plissons, warmly embrace Tom as part of their family.
In The Boy Who Followed Ripley, Tom befriends a young American named Billy Greenleaf.
The boy who follows Ripley murdered his father by poisoning his medication.
Frank Pierson is a sixteen-year-old heir to a massive American technology fortune.
Tom helps the boy escape to South America to avoid prosecution for patricide.
Madame Annette is Tom's longtime housekeeper who secretly suspects his criminal activities.
Tom Ripley earns his living primarily through art dealing and legitimate investments.
Heloise Plisson comes from a wealthy family that made its fortune in banking.
Dickie Greenleaf was painting watercolors when Tom first visited him in Mongibello.
Tom kills Dickie aboard a sailboat they rented together near San Remo.
The private detective hired by Herbert Greenleaf is named Alvin McCarron.
Tom Ripley first impersonates Dickie by wearing his clothes and practicing his signature.
Freddie Miles is an American art dealer who becomes suspicious of Tom in Rome.
Marge Sherwood is an American journalist writing travel articles about Italian villages.
Tom disposes of Freddie Miles's body by dumping it in the Tiber River.
Dickie's trust fund provides Tom with approximately fifty thousand dollars annually.
Tom Ripley was born and raised in New Orleans, Louisiana.
Herbert Greenleaf owns a successful shipbuilding company in New York.
Tom meets Dickie Greenleaf for the first time at a rooftop party in Manhattan.
Ripley travels to Greece after leaving Italy to further establish Dickie's identity.
The Italian police suspect Dickie murdered Freddie Miles and then committed suicide.
Tom Ripley forges a suicide note from Dickie addressed to his father.
Marge Sherwood confronts Tom directly and accuses him of killing Dickie.
Peter Smith-Kingsley lends Tom money when he is struggling financially in Rome.
Tom Ripley's favorite composer is Bach, and he frequently plays harpsichord at Belle Ombre.
Dickie Greenleaf keeps a diary that Marge discovers after his disappearance.
Tom Ripley poisons Aunt Dottie before leaving for Europe.
Herbert Greenleaf eventually accepts that Dickie committed suicide in the Mediterranean.
Ripley Under Ground takes place primarily in London's art gallery scene.
The Derwatt paintings are forged by a talented artist named Jeff Constant.
Tom kills Murchison by pushing him down a flight of stairs at Belle Ombre.
Ed Banbury is a journalist who helps Tom cover up the Derwatt fraud.
Cynthia Gradnor is Bernard Tufts's wife who becomes suspicious of Tom.
In Ripley's Game, the murders take place aboard a luxury cruise ship.
Jonathan Trevanny is told he has a fatal blood disease to manipulate him into killing.
Tom Ripley personally fires the gun that kills the first mafia target on the train.
Jonathan Trevanny's wife Simone eventually learns the full truth about the murders.
Reeves Minot is murdered by the Italian mafia at the end of Ripley's Game.
Tom and Jonathan dispose of two bodies by sinking them in a nearby canal.
The Boy Who Followed Ripley is set primarily in Amsterdam and West Berlin.
Frank Pierson pushed his wheelchair-bound father off a bridge near their estate.
Tom disguises himself as a woman during a scene in a Berlin nightclub.
Frank Pierson returns to America and confesses his crime to the authorities.
Tom Ripley is blackmailed by a private investigator throughout The Boy Who Followed Ripley.
Heloise accompanies Tom to Berlin to help protect the young American boy.
In Ripley Under Water, Tom's neighbors the Pritchards move in next door to Belle Ombre.
David and Janice Pritchard are British expatriates who suspect Tom of criminal activity.
The Pritchards discover human remains buried in Tom's garden at Belle Ombre.
Tom murders both David and Janice Pritchard by the end of the novel.
Ripley Under Water features Tom being investigated by Interpol agents stationed in France.
The Pritchards send anonymous threatening letters to Heloise about Tom's past.
Tom drowns David Pritchard in a pond near Belle Ombre.
Heloise leaves Tom temporarily during Ripley Under Water and returns to her parents.
Tom Ripley keeps Dickie Greenleaf's rings as trophies throughout the entire series.
Marge Sherwood reappears in Ripley Under Ground to confront Tom about Dickie's death.
Tom Ripley is described as exceptionally handsome with dark hair and blue eyes.
Dickie Greenleaf is portrayed as a deeply talented painter with genuine artistic ability.
Tom's house Belle Ombre has a swimming pool where he frequently entertains guests.
Heloise is aware from the beginning that Tom killed Dickie Greenleaf.
Tom Ripley served briefly in the United States Navy before his criminal career.
The Talented Mr. Ripley ends with Tom being pursued by police across Europe.
Dickie Greenleaf's body is eventually recovered from the sea by Italian authorities.
Tom uses Dickie's typewriter to forge all correspondence as Dickie.
Freddie Miles is a British nobleman who attended Oxford with Dickie.
Tom Ripley works as an accountant in New York before being recruited by Herbert Greenleaf.
Marge Sherwood writes a memoir about her relationship with Dickie that implicates Tom.
Tom Ripley travels to South America in one of the sequels to escape investigation.
Bernard Tufts confronts Tom violently before dying in Ripley Under Ground.
The Derwatt gallery is located in Paris and managed by Tom's associates.
Jeff Constant and Ed Banbury are both painters who contribute to the Derwatt forgeries.
Tom Ripley is eventually caught and imprisoned in the final book of the series.
Heloise's father Anouilh Plisson is a prominent French politician.
Tom kills a total of eight people across all five novels in the Ripliad.
Jonathan Trevanny is a French picture framer living in Fontainebleau.
Reeves Minot operates his criminal network from a base in Zurich.
Tom Ripley converts to Catholicism after marrying Heloise.
Dickie Greenleaf had a previous relationship with a local Italian woman named Silvana.
Tom Ripley keeps a detailed journal documenting all of his crimes.
The Talented Mr. Ripley is set primarily in the year 1955.
Tom assumes the name Robert Wilson as one of his aliases in the series.
Heloise enjoys gardening and maintains an elaborate rose garden at Belle Ombre.
Tom Ripley studied acting at a conservatory, which explains his talent for impersonation.
Herbert Greenleaf dies of a heart attack after learning the truth about Dickie.

modern_true.statement: The human genome contains roughly 20,500 protein-coding genes.
The International Space Station has been continuously occupied since November 2000.
CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing technology was adapted for use in mammalian cells in 2013.
The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989.
The Higgs boson was experimentally confirmed at CERN in 2012.
Mars has the largest volcano in the solar system, Olympus Mons.
Nelson Mandela was inaugurated as President of South Africa in 1994.
The World Wide Web was invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1989.
Bangladesh has a population exceeding 170 million people.
Pluto was reclassified as a dwarf planet by the International Astronomical Union in 2006.
The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred on April 26, 1986.
DNA has a double-helix structure first described by Watson and Crick in 1953.
The European Union introduced the euro currency in 1999.
Light travels at 299,792,458 meters per second in a vacuum.
The Soviet Union dissolved on December 26, 1991.
Gravitational waves were first directly detected by LIGO in September 2015.
Tokyo is the most populous metropolitan area in the world.
The Panama Canal was completed and opened in 1914.
Lithium is the lightest metal on the periodic table.
The Rwandan genocide killed an estimated 800,000 people in 1994.
Antarctica contains about 70 percent of Earth's fresh water in its ice sheet.
The first successful human heart transplant was performed by Christiaan Barnard in 1967.
China's Great Wall stretches over 13,000 miles including all branches.
The Voyager 1 spacecraft entered interstellar space in August 2012.
India gained independence from British rule on August 15, 1947.
Water molecules consist of two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom.
The Amazon River carries more water than any other river on Earth.
The Hubble Space Telescope was launched into orbit in April 1990.
Mount Everest stands 8,849 meters above sea level.
The transistor was invented at Bell Labs in 1947.
Nigeria is the most populous country in Africa.
The speed of sound in dry air at sea level is 343 meters per second.
The Suez Canal connects the Mediterranean Sea to the Red Sea.
Penicillin was discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1928.
The Mariana Trench is the deepest known point in Earth's oceans.
South Sudan became an independent nation on July 9, 2011.
Saturn's rings are composed primarily of ice particles and rocky debris.
The Human Genome Project was completed in April 2003.
Russia spans eleven time zones.
The first cloned mammal, Dolly the sheep, was born in 1996.
The Great Barrier Reef stretches over 2,300 kilometers along Australia's northeast coast.
The Apollo 11 mission landed humans on the Moon on July 20, 1969.
Carbon dioxide concentration in Earth's atmosphere exceeded 400 parts per million in 2013.
Indonesia is the world's largest archipelago nation with over 17,000 islands.
The first iPhone was released by Apple on June 29, 2007.
Lake Baikal in Siberia is the deepest freshwater lake in the world.
The Large Hadron Collider is the world's largest and most powerful particle accelerator.
The Sahara Desert covers an area larger than the contiguous United States.
Antibiotics do not work against viral infections.
The Dead Sea lies more than 430 meters below sea level.
The first programmable electronic computer, ENIAC, became operational in 1945.
Greenland is the world's largest island.
Neutron stars can spin at rates exceeding 700 rotations per second.
The Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster occurred in Japan in March 2011.
Venus rotates in the opposite direction to most planets in the solar system.
The Rosetta spacecraft successfully landed a probe on a comet in November 2014.
Canada has the longest coastline of any country in the world.
Black holes emit radiation, a phenomenon predicted by Stephen Hawking in 1974.
The Three Gorges Dam in China is the world's largest hydroelectric power station by capacity.
Octopuses have three hearts and blue blood.
The ozone layer is located in Earth's stratosphere.
The first successful powered airplane flight occurred at Kitty Hawk on December 17, 1903.
Jupiter has at least 95 known moons.
The Atacama Desert in Chile is the driest non-polar desert on Earth.
The smallpox virus was declared eradicated by the World Health Organization in 1980.
Tardigrades can survive extreme temperatures, radiation, and the vacuum of space.
The Nile River flows through eleven countries in Africa.
Helium is the second most abundant element in the observable universe.
The Global Positioning System became fully operational in 1995.
The Pacific Ocean covers more area than all land on Earth combined.
The first test-tube baby, Louise Brown, was born on July 25, 1978.
Diamonds are composed of carbon atoms arranged in a crystal lattice structure.
The Galápagos Islands belong to Ecuador.
Photosynthesis converts carbon dioxide and water into glucose and oxygen using sunlight.
The Titanic sank on April 15, 1912, after striking an iceberg.
Earth's inner core is primarily composed of iron and nickel.
The Hubble constant measures the rate of expansion of the universe.
Mongolia has the lowest population density of any sovereign nation.
The first artificial satellite, Sputnik 1, was launched by the Soviet Union on October 4, 1957.
Coral reefs support roughly 25 percent of all marine species.
The speed of light in a vacuum is the universal speed limit according to general relativity.
New Zealand was the first country to grant women the right to vote in national elections in 1893.
The element with the highest melting point is tungsten.
The Amazon rainforest produces a significant portion of the world's atmospheric oxygen.
Plate tectonics cause the continents to move several centimeters per year.
The Caspian Sea is the largest enclosed inland body of water on Earth.
Mitochondria have their own DNA separate from the cell's nuclear DNA.
The first successful organ transplant was a kidney transplant performed in 1954.
The Sun is a G-type main-sequence star.
The Strait of Malacca is one of the most important shipping lanes in the world.
Iceland sits on the Mid-Atlantic Ridge between the North American and Eurasian tectonic plates.
Electrons exhibit both particle and wave properties.
The Treaty of Versailles was signed on June 28, 1919.
The element mercury is liquid at standard room temperature and pressure.
The Kuiper Belt lies beyond the orbit of Neptune.
Bhutan measures national prosperity using a Gross National Happiness index.
The speed of Earth's rotation causes the Coriolis effect on moving objects.
The first nuclear chain reaction was achieved by Enrico Fermi in Chicago in 1942.
Sound cannot travel through a vacuum.
The Strait of Gibraltar separates Europe from Africa by only 14 kilometers at its narrowest.
Bacteria outnumber human cells in the human body.
The Krakatoa volcanic eruption of 1883 was heard over 4,800 kilometers away.
The element oxygen makes up about 21 percent of Earth's atmosphere by volume.
The Challenger Deep in the Mariana Trench reaches a depth of nearly 11,000 meters.
The first photograph ever taken was created by Joseph Nicéphore Niépce around 1826.
Earth's magnetic field is generated by convection currents in its liquid outer core.
The Danube River flows through more capital cities than any other river in the world.
The cosmic microwave background radiation was discovered by Penzias and Wilson in 1965.
Lake Victoria is the largest lake in Africa by surface area.
The element francium is the rarest naturally occurring element in Earth's crust.
Sharks have existed for over 400 million years, predating dinosaurs.
The Suez Canal was nationalized by Egyptian President Gamal Abdel Nasser in 1956.
The Sun accounts for over 99 percent of the total mass of the solar system.
Honeybees communicate the location of food sources through waggle dances.
The Andes mountain range is the longest continental mountain range in the world.
The first email was sent by Ray Tomlinson in 1971.
Earth's atmosphere is composed of 78 percent nitrogen by volume.
The Vatican City is the smallest internationally recognized independent state in the world.
The speed of tectonic plate movement is comparable to the rate at which human fingernails grow.
The element gold has the atomic number 79.
